title: Synthetic polymer sulphonated polyisoprene as a universal anticoagulant for laboratory testing
abstract:
180
Kinoshita et al.
© 2000 Wiley-Liss, Inc.
Journal of Clinical Laboratory Analysis 14:180–187 (2000)
JCLA 1956
Synthetic Polymer Sulphonated Polyisoprene as a Universal
Anticoagulant for Laboratory Testing
Yoshimitsu Kinoshita, Kensuke Ohta,* Takahisa Yamane, Masayuki Hino,
Takayuki Takubo, Tomohiro Samori, and Noriyuki Tatsumi
Department of Clinical and Laboratory Medicine, Osaka City University Medical School,
Asahimachi, Osaka, Japan
*Correspondence to: Kensuke Ohta, M.D., Ph.D., Department of Clinical
and Laboratory Medicine, Osaka City University Medical School, 1-4-3,
Asahimachi, Abeno-ku, Osaka 545-8585, Japan. E-mail: kenohta@
med.osaka-cu.ac.jp
Received 4 January 2000; Accepted 9 March 2000
To evaluate the feasibility of a synthetic
polymer, sulphonated polyisoprene (SPIP),
to be used as a new laboratory anticoagu-
lant, hematological items were compared be-
tween blood samples anticoagulated with
EDTA and SPIP, as were biochemical and
electrolyte items between serum samples
and SPIP-anticoagulated blood. Among he-
matological items, the average platelet count
of the SPIP group was significantly lower
than that of the EDTA group, due to platelet
aggregation in the SPIP group. Addition of
kanamycin to SPIP, but not of aspirin or pros-
taglandin E1, restored platelet count to about
80% of that in the EDTA group, and in-
creased coefficients of correlation with the
EDTA group for most hematological items
examined. For biochemical and electrolyte
items, high (> 0.95) correlation coefficients
were obtained for 15 of 18 items between the
serum and SPIP-anticoagulated samples.
However, for some of these items, the addi-
tion of kanamycin decreased coefficients of
correlation. Thus, SPIPs are excellent can-
didates for new anticoagulants that can be
used for evaluation of hematological, bio-
chemical, and electrolyte items with a single
test tube in routine laboratory work. How-
ever, some improvements are required for
use of kanamycin as an additive to inhibit
platelet aggregation induced by SPIP, and
in maintaining reliability of biochemical and
electrolyte measurements. J. Clin. Lab. Anal.
14:180–187, 2000.
© 2000 Wiley-Liss, Inc.
Key words:
anticoagulant; sulphonated polyisoprene; synthetic polymer; kanamycin; platelet
aggregation; laboratory testing
INTRODUCTION
Recent developments in laboratory technology and trans-
portation systems have helped reduce the time, cost, and man-
power required for daily blood examination in hospitals.
However, for a long time there has been little change in the
methods used to sample blood specimen; ion chelators such
as ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) and antithrombin
reagents such as heparin have long been employed for hema-
tological evaluation, while serum has been used for blood
chemistry and electrolyte examination. A “universal antico-
agulant” suitable for the measurement of all these items would
be expected not only to decrease the amount of blood needed
to be withdrawn from patients but also to significantly im-
prove the time- and cost-effectiveness of laboratory testing.
Recently, a new synthetic polymer, sulphonated polyiso-
prene (SPIP) was found to have in vitro anticoagulant effects
as a result of activities including that of antithrombin (1).
However, no other significant effects on plasma protein com-
ponents have been reported for SPIP, and SPIP does not in-
clude free ions in its chemical structure. Thus, SPIP also might
be useful for measurement of blood chemistry and plasma
electrolyte components, in addition to measurement of CBC.
However, no trial has assessed the use of SPIP as a laboratory
anticoagulant. In this study, we therefore examined in detail
the feasibility of use of SPIP for assessment of both CBC and
blood chemistry parameters. We also studied the effects of
known inhibitors of platelet aggregation [which is reported
to be induced by SPIP (2)], in order to establish the use of
SPIP as a new anticoagulant.
MATERIALS AND METHODS
Anticoagulants
SPIP was kindly donated by Japan Synthetic Rubber Co.,
Inc. (Tokyo, Japan). SPIP is a negatively-charged water-
soluble polymer with sulphonated groups (1,2) (Fig. 1). The
anticoagulant activity of SPIP has been reported to be due
 Sulphonated Polyisoprene as a Universal Anticoagulant
181
primarily to the concentration of sulphonated groups, but in
part to the molecular weight of SPIP (2). In this study, we
used SPIP with 2.5–4.6 mmol sulphonate group/g with an
average molecular weight of c.57,000 (5,000–80,000) based
on the findings of a previous study, in order to obtain maxi-
mum anticoagulative activity (2). A test tube containing 1.5
mg of SPIP was prepared to hold 5 ml whole blood in order
to obtain a final SPIP concentration of 0.3 mg/ml blood (2).
Vacuum tubes containing EDTA-2K or heparin were pur-
chased from Terumo Co. (Tokyo, Japan).
Inhibitors of Platelet Aggregation
Acetyl salicylate (aspirin), prostaglandin E1 (PGE1), and
kanamycin were purchased from SIGMA, Wako Pure Chemi-
cal Industries (Osaka, Japan), and Meiji Seika Kaisha, LTD
(Tokyo, Japan), respectively. These agents were mixed with
SPIP in test tubes as desired.
Specimens
Venous specimens were obtained, after obtaining informed
consent, from 50 healthy volunteers (27 males and 23 females)
who work in our hospital. The average age of volunteers was
33 years (range: 19–70 years). Blood samples from 30 ran-
domly chosen subjects were used for evaluation of SPIP alone,
while the remaining 20 samples were used for that of SPIP
plus kanamycin (see Results). For assessment of the feasibil-
ity of use of SPIP as a new anticoagulant, EDTA-2K and se-
rum samples were always co-analyzed as controls for
hematological and biochemical/electrolyte items, respectively.
All the blood samples were obtained in the morning before
breakfast and confirmed to exhibit no changes such as hemoly-

sis, coagulation, or fibrin-net formation. Measurements were
performed within 6 hours after venipuncture, except for evalu-
ation of the time courses of change in hematological items.
Hematological, Biochemical and Electrolyte
CBC and the white cell differential were determined using
an NE-8000 (Sysmex Corp., Kobe, Japan). The hematologi-
cal items evaluated in this study and their normal ranges are
summarized in Table 1. The biochemical items were mea-
sured with a fully automated Hitachi 7170 multiple analyzer
(Hitachi Co., Tokyo, Japan). Serum electrolyte concentrations
were measured using a Hitachi 710 (Hitachi Co.). The bio-
chemical and electrolyte items evaluated in this study and
their normal ranges are summarized in Table 2.
For comparison of mean values between experimental
groups, the paired t-test was employed. For analysis of corre-
lation, coefficients of correlation were determined following
TABLE 3. Representative results of hematological analysis of samples anticoagulated with SPIP, SPIP plus PGE1, or SPIP
aWBC, white blood cells; RBC, red blood cells; Hb, hemoglobin; Hct, hematocrit; PLT, platelets; Neu, neutrophils; Lym, lymphocytes; Mon, monocytes;
Eos, eosinophils; Bas, basophils.
Effects of SPIP on cell morphology. Upper pictures show SPIP
effects, and lower pictures show effects of SPIP plus kanamycin (from left
to right: neutrophils, lymphocytes, and platelets). No morphological changes
in white cells were observed with SPIP treatment on Giemsa-stained films,
although platelet activation and aggregation were observed by SPIP alone.
Such platelet aggregates were observed mostly in the feather-end part of the
film. Addition of kanamycin to SPIP prevented the platelet changes induced
by SPIP. Variation of red-cell size among pictures is due to gradient transi-
tion of red cells and differences in site of observation on Giemsa-stained
films. Red-cell distribution width and mean cell volume were the same in
the SPIP and SPIP-plus-kanamycin blood samples.
Sulphonated Polyisoprene as a Universal Anticoagulant
Z-transformation. In both cases, findings of P < 0.05 were
Effects of Inhibitors of Platelet Aggregation on
Blood Cells From the Samples Anticoagulated
Because SPIP was previously reported to induce platelet
aggregation (2), we initially screened for the effects of sev-
eral known inhibitors of platelet aggregation, i.e., aspirin (3–
5), PGE1 (6–7), and kanamycin (8) in addition to SPIP. As
shown in Table 3, this screening test with a small number of
blood samples revealed that the platelet count of samples an-
ticoagulated by SPIP alone was much lower than those of
samples anticoagulated by EDTA. However, kanamycin—but
neither aspirin nor PGE1 in combination with SPIP—signifi-
TABLE 4. Comparision of coefficient of variationa for EDTA,
SPIP, and SPIP plus kanamycin-treated blood samples
aEach value indicates the coefficients of variation (%)  ± SD for 5 repeated
measurements (n = 5). WBC, white blood cells; RBC, red blood cells; Hb,
hemoglobin; Hct, hematocrit; PLT, platelets; Neu, neutrophils; Lym, lym-
phocytes; Mon, monocytes; Eos, eosinophils; Bas, basophils.
Time courses of change in count of hematological items after sam-
pling of blood. The same samples anticoagulated with EDTA, SPIP, or SPIP
with various concentrations of kanamycin were repeatedly measured at vari-
ous time points after venipuncture. During experiments, samples were left
at room temperature. Z, indicate samples anticoagulated with SPIP alone;
4, SPIP plus kanamycin (20 mg/ml); 6, SPIP plus kanamycin (30 mg/ml);
A, SPIP plus kanamycin (40 mg/ml), 5 SPIP plus kanamycin (100 mg/ml).
In all cases SPIP was used at 30 mg/dl. On ordinates, values obtained with
EDTA just after collection are represented as 1. Abscissas indicate times
TABLE 5. Comparison of peripheral blood analysis parameters in EDTA-2K with those in SPIP or SPIP plus
EDTA versus SPIP + kanamycin (n = 20)
aSame sample was measured with different anticoagulants. WBC, white blood cells; RBC, red blood cells; Hb, hemoglobin; Hc, hematocrit; PLT, platelets;
Neu, neutrophils; Ly, lymphocytes; Mono, monocytes; Eos, eosinophils; Bas, basophils.
Coefficients of correlation for peripheral blood analysis param-
eters between EDTA and SPIP or EDTA and SPIP-plus-kanamycin groups.
Horizontal axis indicates values from EDTA-containing samples. Vertical

with SPIP plus kanamycin (2). Thin lines indicate linear correlations be-
tween EDTA and SPIP groups, while thick lines indicate those between EDTA
and SPIP-plus-kanamycin groups. r, coefficient of correration. *, P < 0.0001.
Sulphonated Polyisoprene as a Universal Anticoagulant
cantly restored platelet counts. As shown in Figure 2, SPIP
alone induced significant platelet aggregation, indicating that
low platelet count in SPIP-anticoagulated blood is indeed due
to platelet aggregations. However, formation of platelet
clumps was significantly prevented by addition of kanamy-
cin to SPIP. On the other hand, neither SPIP nor SPIP plus
kanamycin significantly affected the morphology of either
Stability of Measurements of Samples Collected
With SPIP or SPIP Plus Kanamycin
The stability of hematological parameters determined by
five repeated measurements of the same sample anticoagu-
lated with SPIP or SPIP plus kanamycin was determined with
the NE-8000. As shown in Table 4, in most of the items, coef-
ficients of variations (CVs) for the SPIP-alone group were
larger than those for the EDTA group. However, the CVs for
the SPIP plus kanamycin group were nearly the same as those
for the EDTA group, indicating that stable measurement could
be obtained if kanamycin were added to SPIP.
Time Courses of Change in Hematological Items
We also repeatedly measured the same samples anticoagu-
lated with EDTA, SPIP, or SPIP plus various concentrations
of kanamycin at different time points after venipuncture. As
shown in Figure 3, the values of hematological items obtained
with each anticoagulant remained nearly unchanged until at
least 6 hours after venipuncture.
Comparison of Results of Hematological Analysis
Between Blood Samples Anticoagulated With
EDTA and SPIP Alone or EDTA and SPIP Plus
Based on the aforementioned observations, we then in-
creased the number of samples and examined the usefulness
of SPIP or SPIP plus kanamycin as a new universal antico-
agulant. As shown in Table 5, although red cell indices in the
SPIP-alone group were comparable to those in the EDTA
group, platelet counts were much lower than those in the
EDTA group. In addition, the WBC count was slightly lower
in the SPIP group than in the EDTA group. However, WBC
counts in the SPIP plus kanamycin group were comparable to
those in the EDTA group, and platelet counts were restored to
about 80% of those in the EDTA group.
Analysis of Coefficients of Correlation for
Hematological Items Between EDTA and SPIP
Groups or EDTA and SPIP Plus Kanamycin
As shown in Figure 4, when the EDTA- and SPIP-alone
groups were compared, the coefficients of correlation for most
of the hematological items were less than 0.8, indicating that
measurements of CBC using SPIP alone are unstable. On the
other hand, when the EDTA and SPIP-plus-kanamycin groups
were compared, the coefficients of correlation for most of the
hematological items improved to above 0.9.
Analysis of Biochemical and Electrolyte Items
Based on the results of hematological analysis, we exam-
ined whether SPIP and/or SPIP plus kanamycin is suitable
for analysis of biochemical and electrolyte items. As shown
in Table 6, although 10 items in the SPIP group differed sig-
nificantly in value from those in the serum group, all of these
differences, except for that in LDH, were less than 10%. When
kanamycin was added to SPIP, however, 13 of 18 biochemi-
cal/electrolyte items differed significantly from those in the
serum group, and 5 of the 13 items (triglyceride, uric acid,
alanine aminotranferase, lactic dehydrogenase, and alkaline
phophatase) had differences larger than 10%. As shown in
Table 7, 15 of 18 biochemical and electrolyte items had coef-
ficients of correlation between the serum and SPIP-alone
groups higher than 0.95; 2 items (creatinine and K) had coef-
ficients between 0.8 and 0.85, while the coefficient for LDH
was only 0.62. However, when kanamycin was added to SPIP,
the coefficients of correlation for total protein and albumin
and particularly those for electrolyte items were clearly lower
than those between the serum and SPIP-alone groups, sug-
gesting that addition of kanamycin to SPIP may result in in-
stability of measurement of these items.
A series of biopolymers incorporating sulphonated groups
and based on the chemical structure of heparin have been
tested for anticoagulant activity (9). SPIPs were first synthe-
sized by van der Does et al. and were found to have strong
anticoagulant activity (1,10,11). The precise mechanism of
this SPIP activity is not well understood. However, it has been
speculated that the negative charge of the sulphonic residue
of the SPIP polymer inhibits fibrin monomer formation by
antithrombin activity (1,2,10). SPIPs may have advantages
for laboratory use including: 1) water-solubility; 2) biosafety,
as well as ease of handling; 3) no interference with the opera-
tion of presently existing laboratory instruments; 4) no chelat-
ing effect on divalent cations; 5) no free ions, unlike heparin,
EDTA and citric acid compounds; 6) storability for long peri-
ods of time; and 7) cost-effectiveness. However, the major
problem with SPIP, related to evaluation of hematological
items we and others have met (2), is that platelet counts are
much lower than with EDTA, due to platelet aggregation. Also,
in the present study, although SPIP did not significantly af-
fect individual cell morphologies, the coefficients of correla-
tion for WBC and platelets between the SPIP and EDTA
groups were relatively low. In this study, we therefore used a
series of known inhibitors of platelet aggregation to deter-
TABLE 6. Comparison of biochemical and electrolyte items for serum versus SPIP and SPIP plus kanamycin groupsa
Serum versus SPIP + kanamycin (n = 20)
aSame sample was measured with different anticoagulatns. TP, total protein; ALB, albumin; T bil, total bilirubin; TG, triglyceride; T chol, total cholesterol;
BUN, blood urea nitrogen; CRE, creatinine; UA, uric acid; LDH, lactic dehydrogenase; AST, asparaginate aminotransferase; ALT, alanine aminotrans-
ferase; ALP, alkaline phosphatase; LAP, leucine aminopeptidase; γ-GTP, gamma-glutamyltranspeptidase; CHE, cholinesterase; Na, sodium; Cl, chlorine;
mine whether they are capable of inhibiting the aggregation
induced by SPIP. Aspirin is known to inhibit platelet aggre-
gation by inhibiting cyclooxygenase activity (3). PGE1 stimu-
lates adenyl cyclase and inhibits platelet aggregation and
activation (6,7). On the other hand, basic water-soluble anti-
biotics, including kanamycin sulfate, were recently found to
prevent and dissociate EDTA-induced aggregation of plate-
lets in pseudothrombocytopenic subjects, presumably due to
positive charges within their chemical structures (8). In our
screening experiments, we found that aspirin and PGE1 ex-
hibited almost no prevention of platelet aggregation, suggest-
ing that the thromboxane-prostaglandin system does not play
a significant role in the platelet aggregation induced by SPIP.
Notably, by contrast, kanamycin significantly prevented plate-
let aggregation and restored platelet counts to about 80% of
values in the EDTA group. Furthermore, kanamycin plus SPIP
was sufficiently effective in improving the coeffcients of cor-
relation between the EDTA and SPIP groups for most hema-
tological items. Although the reasons why kanamycin
prevents aggregation of platelets were not clarified by the
present study, the positive electric charge of kanamycin might
have helped neutralize interaction among polymer, fibrino-
The finding of high coefficients of correlation between the
serum and SPIP groups for almost all the biochemical and
electrolyte items examined in the present study suggest that
SPIP has minimal effects on blood protein components. Al-
though the average values of 10 of these 18 items in the SPIP
group were significantly different from those of the serum
group, these differences were not due to large differences in
values, but to very high coefficients of correlation. However,
kanamycin plus SPIP appeared to decrease the coefficients of
correlation between the serum and SPIP groups for some bio-
chemical items (total protein and alubmin) and for electro-
lyte items, although it did not decrease the coefficients of
correlation for the items of hematological analysis. In par-
ticular, the instability of electrolyte measurements in the pres-
ence of kanamycin suggests that kanamycin might have
affected the ion-selective electrode of the automatic analyzer
In conclusion, we found in the present study that biopoly-
mers such as SPIP are excellent candidates for new antico-
agulants usable for simultaneous evaluation of hematological,
biochemical, and electrolyte items in routine laboratory work.
To overcome the major disadvantage of SPIP—its induction
of platelet aggregation—addition of basic water-soluble an-
tibiotics to SPIP might be needed. However, some improve-
ments in the additive, as well as in the choice of automated
analyzer, may be required for more reliable evaluation of bio-
chemical and electrolyte items.
Sulphonated Polyisoprene as a Universal Anticoagulant
